reasoning: The informal claim "the improper integral $\lim_{B\to\infty}\int_0^B \sin(x)\sin(x^2)\,dx$ converges" is standardly formalized as the existence of a real number $y$ such that the function $B \mapsto \int_0^B \sin(x)\sin(x^2)\,dx$ tends to $y$ as $B \to \infty$. The Lean statement asserts exactly this using `∃ y : ℝ, Tendsto (fun B => ...) atTop (𝓝 y)`. Using `Set.Ioo 0 B` instead of `[0,B]` is irrelevant since endpoints have Lebesgue measure zero. The integrand `Real.sin x * Real.sin (x ^ 2)` matches $\sin(x)\sin(x^2)$, and no extraneous hypotheses are added or required ones dropped. The formalization is logically equivalent to the informal claim.
